coredata.dc:title: Development of biodegradable films reinforced with silver functionalized cow milk carbon dots for active food packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ananthi, P.
Hemkumar, K.
Subasini, S.
Pius, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: Synthetic food packaging films exhibit poor biodegradability and their use has resulted in significant environmental issues. The objective of this work was to develop organic, biopolymer-based antibacterial packaging films as an alternative to synthetic packaging films. In this study, agar was chosen as a biopolymer matrix, carbon dots (CDs) derived from cow milk served as an antibacterial source and Ag nanoparticles were added to enhance the antibacterial activity. The prepared films were characterized by SEM, TEM, UTM, XRD, TGA-DTA, FT-IR, and water contact angle measurements. The prepared films showed good antibacterial activity against E.coli and S. aureus and a tensile strength value of about 41.85 MPa. In addition to these, the prepared films showed good biodegradable properties of about 86 % degradation in 60 days and good UV barrier properties. Finally, the films were tested for real-time application on sliced tomatoes, which also showed good results even after 4 days at room temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Agar
Carbon dots
Active packaging
Antimicrobial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Agar/Ag-CMCDs based antibacterial film
Antibacterial activity of food packaging films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of Agar/Ag-CMCDs based antibacterial film.
a) SEM micrographs of A0, Ag-CMCDs, A1, A2, A3 and b) EDAX image of A3 film.
TEM images and particle size (inset) of a) CDs b) AgNPs c) Ag- CMCDs d) Agar/Ag-CMCDs (A3) film.
FTIR analysis of A0, Ag-CMCDs, A1, A2 and A3 films.
XRD spectrum of A0, Ag-CMCDs, A1, A2 and A3films.
a) TGA and b) DTG spectra of A0, Ag-CMCDs and A3 films.
Water contact angle measurements of A0, A1, A2 and A3 films.
Water vapour permeability test for A0, A1, A2 and A3films.
Antibacterial effect of A0, A1, A2 and A3 films against Echericha coli, Staphylococcus aureus.
Degradation weight loss of the A0, A1, A2 and A3 films.
Mechanical properties of A0, A1, A2 and A3 films.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Mean values in the same column denoted by different superscripts are significantly (p > 0.05) different by Duncan's multiple range tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TGA and DTG values of A0, Ag-CMCDs and A3 films.
Transparency of the films A0, A1, A2 and A3.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Mean values in the same column denoted by different superscripts are significantly (p > 0.05) different by Duncan's multiple range tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bacterial inhibitory zone for A0, A1, A2, A3, A4 and A5 against E. coli, S. aureus.

head.ce:title: Development of biodegradable films reinforced with silver functionalized cow milk carbon dots for active food packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University. Gandhigram, Dindigul, 624 302, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University. Gandhigram

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute - Deemed to be University. Gandhigram, Dindigul, 624 302, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthetic food packaging films exhibit poor biodegradability and their use has resulted in significant environmental issues. The objective of this work was to develop organic, biopolymer-based antibacterial packaging films as an alternative to synthetic packaging films. In this study, agar was chosen as a biopolymer matrix, carbon dots (CDs) derived from cow milk served as an antibacterial source and Ag nanoparticles were added to enhance the antibacterial activity. The prepared films were characterized by SEM, TEM, UTM, XRD, TGA-DTA, FT-IR, and water contact angle measurements. The prepared films showed good antibacterial activity against E.coli and S. aureus and a tensile strength value of about 41.85 MPa. In addition to these, the prepared films showed good biodegradable properties of about 86 % degradation in 60 days and good UV barrier properties. Finally, the films were tested for real-time application on sliced tomatoes, which also showed good results even after 4 days at room temperature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anitha Pius helped in the supervision of the work.
Ananthi P carried out the experiments and wrote the manuscript.
Hemkumar K conceptualized the present idea.
Subasini S carried the technical work.

Recent developments in active and intelligent packaging technologies have provided a variety of innovative methods for extending the shelf life of food products and improving their quality and safety. The main purpose of food packaging is to preserve the quality and safety of food products during storage and transportation, as well as to prolong their shelf life by avoiding unfavourable factors such as spoilage caused by microorganisms, chemical contaminants, oxygen, moisture, light and external forces. Due to their advantageous mechanical, physical and chemical properties; hydrocarbon polymers like polyethylene, low- and high-density polyethylene and polypropylene have been used as effective materials for packaging applications [1–3]. However, these polymers have undesirable environmental effects and contribute to climate change [4,5]. Biodegradable, ecologically friendly, and bio-based polymers have been presented as viable alternatives to manage and resolve these concerns [6–8]. The development of such packaging materials offers significant economic and environmental advantages because they are biodegradable, biocompatible, edible, and isolated from renewable resources such as algae or plants [9–11]. Several polysaccharides have been searched for their possible use in packaging, including chitosan, agar, starch, carrageenan, guar gum etc [12,13]. Developing biopolymer-based composites has recently been investigated as one of the most promising ways to improve the properties of biopolymers [14–16]. Agar is one such highly attractive polysaccharide for food packaging applications [17]. Agar has various advantages, including low cost, good film-forming capacity, transparency, moderate water resistance and compatibility [18–23]. However, there are still certain limitations to their application in the food packaging industry. First, agar-based materials usually have poor mechanical properties and low thermal stability which can interfere with material processing and handling during production. Second, they lack functionality in terms of antibacterial activity, which can greatly affect food quality and shelf-life. In order to overcome these restrictions and make them suitable for food packing, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted dramatic attention and extensive research interest owing to their unparalleled physical and chemical properties, including tunable photoluminescence, favourable biocompatibility and low toxicity and bacterial activity [24]. These features offer the potential for exciting applications in bio-imaging and sensing, catalysis, packaging, medical diagnosis and pollutant detection but their instability in the environment represents the main drawbacks [25,26]. A facile, environmentally friendly and benign green technique to synthesize CDs utilizing low-cost sources has emerged as an alternative to complex synthesis procedures for commercial viability [27]. Functional groups present in biological metabolites can reduce metal ions and stabilize the synthesized nanoparticles with the additional advantage of enhanced biological activities. It has been demonstrated that CDs were good electron donors, which could be used as a reductant for reducing noble metal ions to form metal/CDs nanoparticles (NPs) [28–30]. Metal nanoparticles, such as silver, copper, zinc, and titanium, have been identified as possessing antibacterial activities; among them, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have shown the most promising potency as a simple, stable, and rapid antibacterial agent. Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs), one of the noble metal nanoparticles, have been used in certain applications such as biological tagging, antimicrobial activity and catalysis [31]. In the current study, Carbon-dot-supported silver nanoparticles (Ag-CMCDs) obtained from cow milk and these Ag-CMCDs NPs the direct reduction of AgNO3 using CMCDs as both the reducing agent and stabilizer, and showed that these could be used as a superior enhanced antibacterial activity. Using the solvent casting technique, a unique Ag-CMCD/Agar antibacterial film was created. The produced nanocomposite antibacterial film is thought to offer excellent promise in uses like food packaging due to its superior antibacterial, light-admitting, and flexible qualities.

Food-grade agar, silver nitrate and glycerol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and Cow milk was purchased in the neighbourhood market in Chinnalapatti, Dindigul District, Tamilnadu, India. Escherichia coli (ATCC 25,922) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25,923) were obtained from (Department of Biology) in our Institute. All of the studies were carried out using double-distilled water.

Cow milk was hydrothermally processed to create CMCDs. In a typical synthesis, deionized water was used to dilute cow milk (10 mL) to 60 mL before the combination was put in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and cooked at 180 °C for 12 h. The big spots were then removed from the CMCDs using centrifugation at 11,000 rpm for 5 min. An CMCDs-containing yellow supernatant was then formed [32].

The preparation of the AgCD nanocomposite was based on previously published literature [33]. In general, mixtures of CD and AgNO3 in ethanol were prepared at a concentration of 2 mg mL −1. Using a 6 W UV lamp (Vilber Lourmat), this solution was placed in a UV quartz cuvette (1 cm × 1 cm × 5 cm) and exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 254 nm for 100 min. Three centimetres were kept between cells and the light source. The creation of the Ag-CMCD nanocomposite is shown by the colour change of the solution from pale yellow to dark brown following the photoreaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Agar/Ag-CMCDs based antibacterial film
ce:section.ce:para: Agar based films incorporated with Ag-CMCDs were prepared by using a simple solution casting method as shown in Fig. 1 [34]. The film solution was prepared by dissolving 3 g of agar in 150 mL of distilled water with 3 drops of glycerol as a plasticizer while mixing vigorously for approximately 25 min at 60 °C for 2 h to obtain a homogenous solution. In order to create different percentages of Ag-CMCDs-incorporated films, different amounts of Ag-CMCDs were likewise added and mixed with respect to the overall mass of homogeneous agar solution. Agar-loaded Ag-CMCDs film-forming solution was uniformly cast using a casting instruments. As a composite film, the resulting film was put inside coagulation bath, cleaned, and dried. The films were stored for 24 h at room temperature in a humidifier with a relative humidity of 50 % in preparation for subsequent use. The prepared films were designated as Agar (A0), Agar- Ag-CMCDs 1 % (A1), Agar- Ag-CMCDs 2 % (A2) and Agar- Ag-CMCDs 3 % (A3) according to the number of Ag-CMCDs.

A FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer) on a JASCO FTIR-460 plus spectrometer in the 4000-400 cm−1 range was used to examine the functional groups on the prepared films. A morphologies were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; model: TECNAI F30) and SEM (scanning electron microscopy). EDAX mapping was used to examine the distribution and composition of the elements on the film (Bruker, Nano GMBH X′ Flash Detector, 5010 model, Germany). Ag-CMCDs in the composite films' crystal structure and content were assessed by XRD characterisation. The thermal stabilities of the resulting films were examined using an SEIKO model TG/DTA 6200 instruments. The films' angles of contact with water were calculated using goniometry (Renhart Instruments Co, USA). The water contact angle was calculated as the average of three measurements. The ASTM D638 universal testing machine (UTM, Tec Sol India) was used to evaluate the mechanical strength of the composite films at a crosshead speed of 5 mm/min. Test films measuring 150 mm 25 mm were used for the testing [35]. A digital screw gauge was used to measure the composite film's thickness at five distinct points, and the average readings were used to determine the thickness of the film.

Using the standard method E96-E95 (ASTM method 1995), the water vapour permeability (WVP) of the films was evaluated [36] with slight modification. The pre-weighed anhydrous calcium chloride was added to the glass vial's head, and the film samples were securely set on top of it. The vials were stored at 75.5 % RH (Relative humidity) and maintained at a temperature of 25 °C in a desiccator filled with a saturated sodium chloride solution. The vials were placed in a desiccator that was tightly closed, and vials weight was measured at an interval of 2 h for 10 h. The formula Eq. (A.1) was used to determine the film WVP.

where S (m2) represents the surface area of the film, A (g/h) is the slope of the linear regression of the weight over time, and (p1 - p2) (kPa) is the difference in partial pressures between inside and outside the cell.

The UV-barrier and transparency of the films were determined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea) by measuring the % light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660), respectively.

According to the literature, biodegradation tests were conducted [37]. Films that were dry were designated as (W0) in reference to that. In a compressed atmosphere, the weighing films (A0, A1, A2, and A3) were placed on the soil for 60 days. With constant additions of water, this arrangement was operated at room temperature. Gravimetric monitoring was used to track changes that were place in the setup. In order to determine an average weight loss (% W L), all films were reweighed (Wt) after being thoroughly cleaned and deposited. Three times were used the aforementioned techniques to calculate the percentage of weight-loss in WL values were applied Eq. (C.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of food packaging films
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli and S. aureus were cultivated in sterilized Luria–Bertani broth and then incubated overnight at 37 °C with a shaking incubator. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) test was performed according to a reported procedure [38], while the survival of organisms was observed by visual inspection. Before testing, the samples were sterilized in nutrient agar and after that the produced mixture was poured onto Petri plates and allowed to solidify. Thereafter the sample microorganisms (5 % of the lower inoculum) were scrubbed on the solidified surface. All films were cut into 10 mm-diameter circular parts, which were then placed on an inoculated agar plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. After incubation, zones of inhibition distance were measured.

Film properties were tested using individually created films in triplicate as replicated experimental units, and the results were given with a mean ± standard deviation. The SPSS statistical programme IBM SPSS statistics 23 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, Ill., USA) was used to follow a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and determined the significant differences between the film properties (p < 0.05) with Duncan's multiple range tests.

The SEM analyses depict the morphology of the prepared films shown in Fig. 2 . The bare agar film (A0) exhibited homogeneous and smooth morphology. The SEM image of Ag-CMCDs shows a spherical morphology supported by silver nanoparticles. Ag-CMCDs were evenly distributed in the agar film, A1, A2 and A3. The films A1, A2 and A3 are of different ratios (1 wt%, 2 wt %, 3 wt %) of Ag-CMCDs blended with agar show different morphologies like smooth, flat surface and without any agglomeration. Ag-CMCDs were equally dispersed in composite films and there are no other impurities by SEM images and related EDX images, which specifically showed the presence of silver, carbon, and oxygen elements.

The presence of the CMCDs, AgNPs, Ag-CMCDs and Agar/AgCMCDs 3 % (A3) films were confirmed using TEM analysis and particle size were analysed using histogram and its lattice fringe are shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM image and size distribution of the CMCDs thus formed are shown in Fig. 3a, which indicated that the CMCDs were monodisperse and spherical, with a size ranging from 1 to 5 nm. However, the size of the CMCDs particles as determined by the particle size distribution histogram was found to be 4.2 nm and the spacing of lattice fringe was 0.204 nm, which is in consistence with the previously [39,40]. Fig. 3b shows the AgNPs was found to be in spherical shape and the average size ranged from 10 to 60 nm. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 3b), inset reveals that AgNPs are of nano sized with an average particle size of 25.26 nm with d spacing of AgNPs is 0.22 nm. Fig. 3c shows that Ag-CMCDs are nearly spherical in shape with narrow size distribution of particles. The size and distinguishing lattice fringes of Ag-CMCDs mostly distributed in a range of 4.28 nm and 0.239 nm respectively as shown in Fig. 3d inset. The Ag-CMCDs were well-distributed on the agar film (A3). It appears more darker in a TEM image because silver has a larger atomic weight than carbon. Ag-CMCDs and other composite films have shown comparable microstructural characteristics.

Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of agar and agar with composite films containing (1, 2, and 3 wt% of Ag-CMCDs). The agar film was a broad absorption band at about 3343 cm−1, which was attributed to the polymer and water's hydrogen-bonding-induced OH stretching vibration. The peak at 1460 cm−1 was attributed to vibrations of the N–H bending and C–N stretching [41]. The symmetrical deformations of the CH2 group are thought to be contributing to the peak at 1359 cm−1. The polysaccharide OCO and CO tensile vibrations were associated with the peaks at 1000-1180 cm−1. These functional groups may assist AgCDs become more hydrophilic and more stable in aqueous solutions [21].

Agar composite films with 1 wt%, 2 wt%, and 3 wt% Ag-CMCDs have FTIR spectra that are the same to those of the agar only film and do not exhibit an extra peak. This suggests that there was no chemical interaction or contact between the agar and Ag-CMCDs. Peak intensity was raised, nevertheless, when the proportion of Ag-CMCDs in the agar film was raised. The conclusions proved that Ag-CMCDs have been good compatibility with agar polymers.

Fig. 5 exhibits the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of blended films based on agar (A0), agar/Ag-CMCD at 1 %, 2 %, and 3 %, and agar/Ag-CMCD at 3 %. Indicating the amorphous nature of the agar film, the control film showed a peak at 19.0° and a minor shoulder at 13.5°. Ag-CMCDs have distinctive peaks at 2 = 19.30° connected to the (100) plane of CDs as well as peaks at 2 = 38.28°, 44°, 64°, and 77°, which are of the (111), (200), (220), and (311) lattice planes of Ag crystallised in a face-centered cubic network [42]. The XRD pattern of the Ag-CMCDs is in good agreement with that reported in the literature [43–45]. XRD spectra of Agar/Ag-CMCDs composites show characteristic peaks of Ag-CMCDs in A1, A2 and A3 films, proving Ag-CMCDs have good compatibility with agar. The incorporation of Ag-CMCDs did not change the peak position of the agar because of the lower concentration of Ag-CMCDs in the agar films (seeTable 1).

Table 2
shows results in determining the mechanical features of the agar and agar/Ag-CMCD composite films based on thickness, tensile strength (TS), elastic modulus (EM), and elongation at break (EAB). The thickness of films varied from 60.43 ± 0.85 μm to 71.92 ± 0.64 μm as the concentration of Ag-CMCDs had been increased from 1 % to 3 %. The concentration of the added Ag-CMCDs exhibited significant effects on the thickness of the agar films. Similar results were also observed for agar/GSE and agar/gelatin/GSE composite films [46,47].

The agar and agar/Ag-CMCD (1 %) films possess maximum TS of 48.56 ± 0.54 and 46.31 ± 0.96 MPa, respectively. However, the incorporation of Ag-CMCDs at higher concentrations significantly decreased the tensile strength of the agar films (p < 0.05). AgNP dramatically reduced the tensile strength of agar films, which had previously been 47.34 ± 0.73 MPa, to 34.64 ± 2.03 MPa. The reason for this decrease in tensile strength may be attributed to a decrease in interaction molecules between the agar polymer chains and AgNP [24,48–50]. These results indicate that Ag-CMCDs are incompatible with biopolymers, resulting in strong interactions between them. The density and distribution of intra- and intermolecular contacts between the polymer chains in packing films tend to be strongly correlated with the mechanical properties of the films. The elastic modulus (EM) and elongation at break (EAB) revealed the inherent stiffness and flexibility of the film, respectively. According to Table 2's findings, agar films exhibited higher EM values than agar/Ag-CMCD composite films (1015.3 ± 88.3 MPa), and the addition of Ag-CMCDs significantly decreased the EM of agar films (p < 0.05). The result correlates with the tensile strength measurements. In contrast, the incorporation of the Ag-CMCDs considerably (p < 0.05) raised the EAB of the agar films. Agar film containing 3 % Ag-CMCDs showed the highest value (25.38 ± 1.86 %). The mechanical characteristics were not significantly changed by the addition of Ag-CMCDs up to 3 %, but after the additions of Ag-CMCDs to the agar films affect the films' mechanical properties.

Thermogravimetric analysis of the Ag-CMCDs is shown in Fig. 6 . In the first stage, 30–100 °C, similar behaviour was observed for the three materials. A decrease of approximately 3.48 % in the initial mass was observed owing to the evaporation of superficial water. In the second stage, between 100 and 300 °C, the Ag-CMCDs exhibited a total weight loss of approximately 23.37 %. In the third and final stages, at approximately 300–800 °C, a weight loss of approximately 27 % was observed in the case of Ag-CMCDs. The figure shows a total loss of approximately 55 % mass for carbon dots by the removal of more stable oxygen-containing functional groups [51,52].

In thermogravimetric measurements, the composite films A0 and A3 showed 2 and 3 steps of weight reduction, respectively. The loss of weight at temperatures between 30 °C and 100 °C is undoubtedly caused by the evaporation of moisture, while the weight loss for A0 at temperatures between 100 °C and 410 °C is caused by the thermal degradation of the plasticizer (glycerol) in the composite films [53,54]. At 350–800 °C, Film A3 observed large weight losses which had been caused by the agar polymer losing organic functional groups. The removal of more stable oxygen-containing functional groups led to the CDs to lose the most weight at a temperature range between 350 and 800 °C (55 %) at the time. According to the results, the composite films were more thermally resistant than the pure agar films. Ag-CMCDs could enhance insulating properties or more resistance to the mass transfer of volatile products produced by temperature changes during film storage. Agar polymers had previously been shown to undergo a similar heat breakdown [55,56].

The transparency and UV-barrier properties of the films were determined by measuring the light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) and 280 nm (T280), respectively. The agar films had high transparency to visible light and somewhat low transmittance to UV light (Fig. S1). The Agar films showed UV-barrier properties with less than 50 % of UV light transmission (T280). The addition of Ag-CMCDs could significantly reduced the T280 values but slightly decreased the T660 values of A1, A2 and A3 films (Table 3 ). The UV-barrier property of the agar based films increased significantly by adding Ag-CMCDs without sacrificing the transparency of the film. Similar UV protection effects have been reported for agar films containing nanoparticles, carrageenan/gelatin based films reinforced with mCDs, and gelatin/carrageenan-based films containing sulphur derived carbon dots [57–61]. UV-barrier is an important property of a packaging film that can preserve the quality of foods that are easily deteriorated by photocatalytic oxidation, such as high-fat foods.

A penetration of moisture and flow through the material can be determined using Water vapour permeable (WVP) study shown in Fig. 7 . The results for the agar and composite films showed that the WVP of agar film was 0.89 g mm/(kPa h m2) whereas it slowly increased to 1.41 g mm/(kPa h m2) for Ag-CMCDs incorporated agar film. As addition of Ag-CMCDs percentage was increased, the WVP value changed from 1.41 to 1.64 g mm/(kPa h m2), but the hydrophilic activity of Ag-CMCDs composite films have no change. The hydrophilic nature of Ag-CMCDs, which preferentially absorb moisture may be the probable reason. In this study, a water vapour channel was constructed on the porous Ag-CMCDs nanoscale, and as more Ag-CMCDs were added, new water vapour channels were produced that were much better compatible with water vapour transfer [62].

The water contact angle generally decreases over time when a tiny drop of water is put on a film surface, because of the vaporisation and intake of water [63]. The wettability and hydrophobicity of the surface of both agar and agar/Ag-CMCDs films were estimated by observing the WCA of the films as can be seen in Fig. 8 . The hydrophobicity of the agar film was significantly affected by the addition of Ag-CMCDs. The incorporation of Ag-CMCDs had an intense effect on the hydrophilicity of the prepared composite films owing to the hydrophilic nature of the Ag-CMCDs. The agar film exhibited a wider water contact angle (78°) than the A1, A2 and A3 composite films. A gradual decrease in the WCA were observed by increasing the concentrations of Ag-CMCDs in the agar films.

The inhibiting impact of A0, A1, A2 and A3 films against common bacterial strains such as Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and (Escherichia coli) E. coli are shown in Fig. 9 . The bacterial effect was evaluated based on clear and disc measuring zone. The A0 film did not have this inhibiting effect. Compared to A0, the inhibitory zones for A1, A2 and A3 films were much higher against S. aureus and E. coli. The presence of Ag-CMCDs in agar films (A1, A2 and A3) enhanced their antibacterial activity. The antibacterial activity of films A1, A2 and A3 is stronger than that of film A0, making them potential packaging materials for food preservation. Table 4 shows the inhibition zones of the different films against (S. aureus), and (E. coli) bacteria.

The various composite films, A0, A1, A2 and A3 degradation rates were observed for ninety days under buried soil for themselves circumstances. The setup's atmospheric condition was uniformly maintained. Fig. 10 illustrates the percentage of loss of weight in each film. The initial weight of the films and the percentage of weight loss were related. The result showed after 60 days the pure A0 film degrades (30 wt %), whereas the Ag-CMCDs-incorporated films A1, A2 and A3 showed 55, 69, and 86 wt % degradations, respectively, for the same period. Compared to the A0, A1, A2 and A3 films, the films with higher % weight loss were significantly and more biodegradable.

In prepared composite films were tested using tomato slices of packaging for applications. Fresh tomato slices with a high moisture content were wrapped in various composite films (A0, A1, A2 and A3) and stored for five days at ambient temperature with 50 % RH, as displayed in Fig. S2. A tomato slice wrapped with the film A0 becomes shrink with a microbial attack on its outerlayer of very next day. After adding a antimicrobial agent, films (A1, A2 and A3) resulted in minimal water loss. The films with A1, A2 and A3 looked comparatively fresh with slight moisture loss and also the absence of microbial attack. These results indicate that the polysaccharide film covering delayed the decay of tomatoes and showed a prolonged shelf life.

Antimicrobial food packaging films were developed by adding Ag-CMCDs as antibacterial agents at different percentages into agar films. Remarkable changes in the transparency, WVP, WCA and mechanical properties were observed. The presence of Ag-CMCDs within the agar film was confirmed by FT-IR and XRD. Changes in the microstructures of the agar/Ag-CMCDs films were confirmed by SEM and TEM. XRD and TGA techniques showed that the thermal stability and crystallinity of the agar-based films were not affected during the synthesis. The agar/Ag-CMCDs composite films exhibited strong antimicrobial activity against both gram-positive and gram-negative food-borne pathogens. The concentration of Ag-CMCDs and bacterial strains is responsible for the antibacterial activity. The Ag-CMCDs trapped by a polymer might be liberated following replacement with molecules of water to produce antimicrobial action. Antibacterial agents can be effectively transported through agar. In conclusion, agar/Ag-CMCDs films are able to be developed as sustainable and biodegradable packaging materials with good antibacterial activity, potentially improving the quality of food to be packed and extending shelf life. However, further studies are required to determine the film's potential performance and improved properties for industrial use.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtsust.2023.100609.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antimicrobial nanoparticles and biodegradable polymer composites for active food packaging applications
Compr. Rev. Food Sci. Food Saf.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Omerovic, M. Djisalov, K. Zivojevic, M. Mladenovic, J. Vunduk, I. Milenkovic, N. Knezevic, I. Gadjanski, J. Vidic, Antimicrobial nanoparticles and biodegradable polymer composites for active food packaging applications, Compr. Rev. Food Sci. Carbohydr. Polym.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Roy, P. Ezati, J.W. Rhim, Gelatin/Carrageenan-Based Functional Films with Carbon Dots from Enoki Mushroom for Active Food Packaging Applications, ACS Appl. Polym. Mater. 3 (2021) 6437-6445. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsapm.1c01175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improvement of active chitosan film properties with rosemary essential oil for food packaging
Int. J. Food Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Abdollahi, M. Rezaei, G. Farzi, Improvement of active chitosan film properties with rosemary essential oil for food packaging, Int. J. Food Sci. Technol. 47 (2012) 847-853. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2621.2011.02917.x.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physical and antimicrobial properties of Gelidium corneum/nano-clay composite film containing grapefruit seed extract or thymol
J. Food Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.O. Lim, S.A. Jang, K. Bin Song, Physical and antimicrobial properties of Gelidium corneum/nano-clay composite film containing grapefruit seed extract or thymol, J. Food Eng. 98 (2010) 415-420. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfoodeng.2010.01.021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot-polymer nanoporous membrane for recyclable sunlight-sterilized facemasks
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Singh, N. Shauloff, C.P. Sharma, R. Shimoni, C.J. Arnusch, R. Jelinek, Carbon dot-polymer nanoporous membrane for recyclable sunlight-sterilized facemasks, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 592 (2021) 342-348. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.02.049.
